videos.title: 2007 Lemurian race start
videos.description: Start of the 2007 Shasta Lemurian mountain bike race held in Whiskeytown, California.

videos.title: S.C.A.R.S. (PS) Music - Canyon
videos.description:
S.C.A.R.S. (Super Computer Animal Racing Simulation) was released in 1998 for PlayStation, Nintendo64 and PC.
The music is composed by Gregory Heath.

videos.title: Cross-country: a sport for true athletes
videos.description:
Lungs and legs of a champion and perfect equipment are the elements needed to win in the cradle of Italian cross-country, the stadium of Lago di Tesero in Val di Fiemme. This is the place where the medals of the Nordic World Ski Championships were awarded to the best champions of this discipline. Today, the ski-tracks of Val di Fiemme will decide who is the toughest cross-country skier of the Winter Universiade Trentino 2013. We followed them during an evening training: it wasn't easy but we'd like to share with you this experience.
Take a look to the videos of all sports disciplines of Winter Universiade Trentino 2013.
Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/WUTrentino2013/app_240090246155106

videos.title: Norway’s Nordic Dynasty | A Century of Olympic Dominance
videos.description:
🎿 The Nordic Dynasty | A Century of Norwegian Olympic Dominance
For Norway, Olympic gold is not ambition. It is expectation.
The most dominant program in Winter Olympic history.
Subtitles available in Norwegian.

In this episode of Hall of Teams, host Kirk Jensen explores how Norway built an Olympic machine through skiing, science, and ruthless internal competition.

In a country shaped by mountains, forests, and snow, skiing was never a sport added later. It was survival, movement, and identity. Over time, it became something more, a national language spoken through tracks cut into winter.

From early pioneers racing on birch and hickory, to the evolution of wax science, technique, and selection standards that leave champions at home, this is the story of how Norway turned skiing into a system, and dominance into tradition.

Every Olympic cycle, the hardest race is not against the world, but against teammates. Selection is brutal. Standards are unforgiving. Wearing the Norwegian colors means carrying the weight of a century of results.

Relive the moments that defined Olympic skiing, the athletes who raised the bar, and the mindset that made medals feel inevitable. For Norway, the Olympics are not about surprise. They are about proof.
⏱️ In this Episode
00:00 Intro: The Nordic Dynasty
01:15 Episode Road Map
01:54 Chapter 1: The Architecture of Winter
04:11 Crest and Colors
05:25 Chapter 2: The Black Art of Wax
08:14 Chapter 3: When Wood Gives Way
10:14 Trivia from the Hall - The Bronze That Took 50 Years
11:09 Chapter 4: The Cradle of Snow
13:25 Behind the Bench
15:13 Chapter 5: Cracks in the Ice
17:16 Trivia from the Hall - The Prince’s Legacy
18:23 Chapter 6: The Norwegian Standard
20:12 Chapter 7: Milano Cortina 2026
21:58 Induction into the Hall of Teams
23:42 Next Time - Ferrari Racing Team

Every inducted team earns its place on the Hall of Teams Wall of Honor.
👉 Vote for your favorite episode this season.
The team with the most likes, comments, and subscribers will be crowned Team of the Year.

🌍 Related Hall of Teams Episodes

🏒 Team Canada Men’s Olymdpic Hockey Team 
The most unforgiving jersey in sport. From early amateur champions to the modern NHL era, discover how a nation learned that Olympic gold is not a dream. It is a responsibility stitched in red and white.
👉 https://youtu.be/dFXtMfKE6Gg

🏉 New Zealand All Blacks
Rugby’s most dominant force. From the Originals of 1905 to the modern era of the Haka, discover how a nation of five million built a sporting myth stitched in black.
👉 https://youtu.be/GatsGnmEZrE

⚽ Sheffield FC
The world’s oldest football club, founded in 1857. Before billion-dollar leagues, football began with a group of cricketers rewriting the game.
👉 https://youtu.be/bpJBrDHtu6Y

🔵🔴 FC Barcelona
More than a club. From La Masia to Camp Nou, see how identity, defiance, and genius turned football into art.
👉 https://youtu.be/_aGzInll4dk

🟢🟡 Australian Baggy Greens
More than a team. From colonial pitches to the MCG, discover how tradition forged the most demanding jersey in cricket.
👉 https://youtu.be/HQ72-iev9W0

📖 About Hall of Teams
Hall of Teams is where history’s greatest sports teams are remembered, honored, and brought to life. Each episode blends cinematic storytelling, verified history, and immersive sound design, crafted to rival the best sports documentaries.

🏆 Across All Sports. Across Time. Across the World.
🙌 Support the Show
Your engagement is what grows the Hall.

videos.transcript:
[cheering]
It's better to go skiing and think of
God than to go to church and think of
sport.
Welcome to the Hall of Teams, the
podcast where sports legends live again.
I'm your host, Kirk Jensen, and today we
journey north to a land carved by
fjords, narrowed by mountains, where
skis cut lines older than roads. Forests
run for miles without interruption.
Darkness lingers, silence carries. This
is not a place that hosts winter. It
belongs to it. Where other teams chase
metals, the red train of Norway produces
expectation.
In the distance, Bulman Collin stands
watch. A landmark carved into the trees.
Below it, the trails fall away. Threads
of snow waiting for motion. This is not
where skiing is learned. This is where
it belongs. And from here, everything
begins. We start at the roots of the
tracks where skiing is survival before
it is sport. Then we move to 1924 when
Norway sets [music] the standard on the
world stage. to Idrita where skiing
becomes a way of life and a national
system. We follow the modern era as
rivals close the gap and Norway is
forced to defend its throne. We look at
the stars per Milano Cartina 2026 and
finally we reach induction into the Hall
of Teams.
And now the story [music] of the most
dominant and decorated team in Winter
Olympic history.
Chapter 1, the architecture of winter.
If you want to understand why Norway
owns the podium, you don't look at a
gym. You look at a map. In the 1800s,
for most of the world, winter is a
season to be survived. It is a period of
stasis, a closing of the doors. But in
the high altitudes of the north, winter
is the only time the world truly opens
up. Before the road, there's the ski
sport, the track. Before the car, there
is a long-distance stride. In the 19th
century, cross country skiing isn't a
sport. It's the postman's route through
a blizzard. It's the doctor's midnight
transit across a frozen plateau. It is
the hunter's only hope of outlasting his
prey. It isn't brand. It is a biological
necessity. But then something shifts.
The utility of movement begins to evolve
into a pursuit of pure relentless speed.
The pioneers of the north stopped asking
how to [music] survive the snow and
start asking how to conquer it. They
move away from heavy cumbersome planks
towards specialized wood that allows for
a faster kick and glide. They take a
survival skill and give it a signature,
the long lung. There was one area where
the Norwegian was truly an artist. The
endurance of the trail.
By the time the calendar turns to the
1880s, the habit begins to organize. In
the capital of Christristiana, modernday
Oslo, the first ski clubs are born. By
1892, energy converges on a landmark
that becomes a global cathedral. Holm.
While the world sees the iconic
silhouette of a hill, the Norwegians see
the forest surrounding it. Holm becomes
the gateway to Nordmarker. Thousands of
miles of trail where the famil grueling
50 kilometer test is born. Here the
culture decides what it truly admires.
The grit of the person who can disappear
in those woods and hold pace for hours.
Once a culture treats world class
endurance as normal daily routine. You
don't need a miracle to win. You just
need to find your rhythm in the tracks.
Cresten colors. The Norwegian kit is a
suit of modern armor rooted in ancient
heraldry. The crest is a red shield with
no in white at the top. A clear country
marker rather than a club style badge.
Across the middle, the blue stripe with
white borders mirrors their Norwegian
flag. So the uniform reads as Norway
instantly, even at speed. And at the
base sits the snow crystal, sharp,
geometric, and centered. a heraldry and
modern Nordic design cue that ties the
crest to winter. The colors red, white,
and blue come from the Norwegian flag,
and the flag starts with history. For
centuries, Norway is tied to Denmark, so
red and white come from the Danish flag.
In 1821, Norway's parliament adopts a
new flag credited to Frederick Meltzer.
He keeps the red and white cross as a
link to Denmark. He adds the blue cross
to align Norway with a red, white, and
blue tradition used by constitutional
democracies of the time and to signal an
independent national unity. The red of
the flag becomes the base color for the
uniform and it fits the nickname rivals
Noel, the red train.
Chapter 2, the black art of wax.
It is 1924 and the world is arriving in
Shemany, France for the first Winter
Olympics. To the central Europeans,
skiing is a novelty, a playground for
the wealthy. But when the Norwegian team
steps off the train, they look like
workers. They carry skis made of solid
birch and hickory, handcarved in valley
workshops, treated not as sports
equipment, but as an extension of their
own legs. The star is Thor Hog, the
personification of the Norwegian
machine. He arrives with [music] 10,000
km of volume in his legs and no modern
coach, allowing Hog to execute a total
lockout, winning the 18K, the 50K, and
the Nordic combined. In Shaman, the
world discovers that while the rest of
Europe is learning how to stand on skis,
the Norwegians are learning how to
breathe through them. This early
dominance isn't just about heart. It is
about the black art of wax. In the
1920s, the secret to speed isn't found
in a lab. It is found in boiling pots in
backyards. Every team has a volatile
recipe of pine tar, resin, beeswax, and
secret additives like fats or bike
tires. Get the wax wrong and you die in
the tracks. You lose momentum to the
friction of the snow. Get it right and
you have a kick that defies gravity. The
message is clear. To beat Norway, you
have to outwork a culture that treats a
50 km race as a standard Sunday
afternoon. As the woods ski era matures,
the golden triangle forms between
Norway, Sweden, and Finland. A 50-year
war of attrition where gold medals are
traded like currency. The rivalry
becomes an arm race of organic
materials. Whose wood is lightest? Whose
tar is stickiest? A brutal age of
endurance where the winner isn't
necessarily the one with the most
talent. It is the one with the highest
tolerance for pain. By 1936, cross
country skiing moves from survival skill
to a weapon of national identity. And
Norway is no longer just a country on a
map. It is the standard by which every
other winter nation is measured. If you
are listening on your favorite podcast
platform, you can also watch this
episode on YouTube with rare photos,
historic match footage, and visuals that
bring the story to life. Search Hall of
Teams on YouTube to find it. And if you
enjoyed the episode, please like,
comment, and subscribe. Your support
helps the channel grow.
Chapter 3. When wood gives way.
If the 1920s are the birth of the sport,
the 1950s are its industrial revolution.
This is the era of the lumberjack
athlete personified by giants from
Norway, Sweden, and Finland. These men
don't train in the modern sense. They
work. Hauling timber, swinging axes in
the frozen forests of their country.
These ironmen are capable of inhuman
suffering, finishing races with fevers,
coughing blood into snow, the ultimate
evidence that the Nordic machine is
built on the refusal to quit. Racing
with their skeletons as much as their
muscles. Through the 1960s, the sport
remains a primal battle of organic
materials. The black art of waxing
becomes a national obsession. But while
the men are made of iron, their
equipment is still made of trees. That
creates a ceiling for speed that can
only be broken by a fundamental shift in
physics, moving the sport away from the
forest floor and into the laboratory.
The world changes forever at the 1974
World Championships in Falloon, Sweden.
The moment the wood ski era takes its
final breath, a young Swede, Thomas
Magnuson, appears with loud, unnervingly
fast skis made of fiberglass and
plastic. That day, the podium tells two
stories. Norway's Magna Mmo wins the 15K
gold on wood, the last person in history
to do so. But the future belongs to
synthetics. The muffled thud of wood is
replaced by the high-pitch wor of
plastic. And the Norwegian machine
realizes that to stay on top, it can no
longer just be the strongest. But it
also has to be the smartest.
Trivia from the hall. The bronze that
took 50 years. Thorleaf Hog leaves
Shamin 1924 as the king of skis. three
gold medals and what everyone believes
is a ski jumping bronze on top of it.
Decades later, a Norwegian ski historian
rechecks the ski jumping scores. A
clerical error surfaces. The bronze
should not have been awarded to Hog. It
belongs to an American, Anders Hogan,
who actually finishes ahead on points.
In Oslo on September 12th, 1974, the
correction finally becomes official. At
a special ceremony, Hog's daughter
presents the bronze medal to 85-year-old
Hogan. A rare Olympic moment where
history is fixed. She tells him, "If my
father had lived, he would have been
very happy to hand over this medal to
you." This is Norwegian sportsmanship.
They honor the truth, even when it means
giving back an Olympic medal.
Chapter 4. The cradle of snow.
At the summer Olympics, the medal table
reads like global superpowers. But when
the world turns to snow and ice, the map
of power flips. Norway, a nation of just
over 5 million people, lives at the top.
Their edge starts with an obsession with
the basics, a philosophy. Old-fashioned
is not old. Old-fashioned is perfect.
This is fueled by the world's largest
sovereign wealth fund, climbing toward
20 trillion croners, roughly $1.8
trillion,
over $320,000
for every citizen. A financial bedrock
that pays for facilities, coaching, and
science, giving athletes the freedom to
develop without turning sport into a
desperate financial escape plan. The
deeper truth is Norway's advantage is
about wasting less, not just spending
more. Their system starts in the shadows
of local schools. Kids ski under
headlamps because winter is not an
event. It is life. In 2007, Norway
codifies this into a revolutionary
document. Children's rights in sport
built on a single word, Idrata, the joy
of sport. In Norway, they don't cut late
bloomers. They don't rank 10-year-olds.
They don't crown national champions in
elementary school. They treat athlete
retention as a national strategy.
Keeping kids in the sport as long as
possible so talent isn't lost to an
early growth spurt or one bad season.
When the elite finally rise, they enter
into the Olympia toppen, the high
performance hub where training is craft
and data is language. But even at the
summit, culture is enforced as strictly
as heart rates. As Olympic legend Kil
Jun put it, "You don't need to be a jerk
to be a great athlete. And on this team,
they simply won't allow it. The pipeline
produces a collective that believes it
represents a way of life. Norway's
dominance stops looking like magic and
starts looking like design."
Behind the bench reveals the leadership,
coaching structure and high performance
support staff that make championship
performance possible.
At the top, the Norwegian Ski Federation
senior leadership sets direction and
appoints the leadership group. The
National Team Director for Cross
Country, often referred to as a lens
luck shave, runs selection planning,
athlete plans, staff hiring, and the
final Olympic nominations. Assistant
directors support training and
international racing coordination. Team
operations handle camps and travel,
hotels and accreditation, plus security
and daily logistics. A director of
athlete performance runs athlete
monitoring and benchmarking, stays in
communication with athletes and their
personal [music] coaches, and supports
selection decisions through the
readiness tracking. On snow, the head
coach owns the training model, pacing
plans, relay strategies, and day-to-day
execution.
Assistant coaches cover sprint,
distance, relay preparation, and
technique and testing support.
Performance staff, medical, strength,
nutrition, recovery, readiness. Then
comes the [music] edge that defines
modern Norwegian cross country, the WAX
team. 12 to 15 full-time specialists,
more than 400 pairs of skis maintained
for race day, and 30 to 50 test runs to
lock in the wax of the day. Often
supported by a customuilt wax truck
valued at more than $1 million. In pure
numbers, the roster typically lands at
16 athletes, usually eight men and eight
women. Around that core, the full
traveling group is often 45 to 60 when
you include coaching, operations,
performance staff, and the full wax
service crew.
Chapter 5. Cracks in the ice.
If Norway's story is only gold medals,
it is too clean to be real. Every
dynasty meets a trial that threatens not
just the record, but identity. In the
mid2010s, the Norwegian machine hits a
storm. Beginning with Martin Yunstrob
Sunbe's case, Sunbi, the reigning titan
of the World Cup, is sanctioned for
improper use of asthma medication.
Then comes the nebulizer controversy.
The team uses medical grade nebulizers
as a recovery tool for healthy athletes.
To the world, it looks like a gray area.
to Norway. It's a PR nightmare that
strikes at the heart of Idrla, the joy
and integrity of sport. The breaking
point arrives in 2016
with Thrice Yohog. Yohog tests positive
for cluster ball, a trace amount from an
Italian lip balm used to treat sun
[music] lips during high altitude
training. In a country where the skiers
are the moral compass, the sight of
their golden girl in a doping hearing
feels like a national trauma. Yohog
appears before cameras, grief on her
face, and delivers a line that shatters
invincibility.
I am completely devastated. It is so
unfair and undeserved, although I am
aware of my responsibility as an
athlete. When the CAS hands Yohog an
18-mon suspension, it sidelines the best
female skier for Pyongqeng 2018. For the
first time in the modern era, Norway is
defensive. Sweden and Finland see a king
that can bleed. This era proves
something. A dynasty isn't defined by
how it wins when the wax is perfect. It
is proven by what it does when the
system is questioned. Rebuilding the
black art of the trail. One kilometer at
a time. Trivia from the hall. The
prince's legacy.
If you want to see a nation's history
written in the snow, you look at the
Burken Biner. It isn't just a 54 km race
of endurance. It is an 800-year-old
rescue mission that never ends. The race
follows the winter route from 1206, a
time of brutal Norwegian civil war. Two
elite skiing warriors, the Burkabiners,
carry the infant prince, Hakun Hawinson,
to safety across the mountains,
shielding him from rivals who want the
throne to honor his weight at the time
he was carried over the mountains. Every
participant carries a 3.5 kg backpack
for the entire 54 km. Cross the finish
line without it and you are
disqualified. The name Burkener comes
from birch legs. So poorly equipped they
wrap birch bark around their shins for
insulation and protection. Underdogs who
saved a king. Today their legacy is a
global pilgrimage. In 2025 over 9,000
skiers take to the trail from over 50
countries.
Chapter 6. The Norwegian standard.
How do you answer a crisis in Norway?
You answer with an unbreakable lineage
of greatness in Pyongqang. Despite
controversy, the Norwegian machine
rewrites the physics of the Winter
Games. 39 medals, 14 delivered by cross
country alone. At the center is Marriott
Burgan, the most decorated Winter
Olympian in history, 15 Olympic medals,
114 [music]
World Cup wins. Yet she remains the
embodiment of Norwegian quiet.
It is hard to understand that I have won
so many medals. When I am still active,
I just look forward to the next race.
Bjorggan stands on the shoulders of
giants like Bejorn Daily, defining the
1990s era of endurance. Daily carries an
engine that pushes known limits of human
biology. His V2 max is an astonishing
96.0, zero. A furnace compared to the
average fit male whose V2 max might
approach 45.
He doesn't just compete. He chases the
pantheon of sports.
It would be quite nice to pass Carl
Lewis. He retires with eight Olympic
golds and does exactly that. Proof the
system isn't just technique. It is a
biological standard. The Norwegian team
is the evidence that even when the ice
cracks, the foundation of the red train
is built on something deeper than a
single race. A century of relentless
documented excellence.
Chapter 7. Milano Cartina 2026.
As the world looks toward Milano Cartina
in 2026, the athletes are being tested
by the most brutal internal competition
in the sports history. In Norway, making
the Olympic team is harder than winning
an Olympic gold medal. They call it the
Norwegian Hunger Games. By January 2026,
the pressure hits fever pitch. While
other nations struggle to find one medal
contender, Norway leaves world champions
at home. Leading the charge is Johanna's
Hurstflute Clayoo. Fresh off a
record-breaking fifth tour to ski title
in January 2026.
Claybu isn't just chasing medals. He is
chasing the Pantheon. He enters with one
goal, a clean sweep of all six events.
Behind him is Harold Adamson, the 2024
World Cup overall champion built for
oxygen starved climbs of the Italian
Elves. On the woman's side, the torch
passes to stars like Christine Stovoske,
the sprint queen who lives to silence
Swedish crowds and Caroline Simpson
Larson who fights through the grueling
2026 trials. The rivals Sweden and
Finland keep pushing because beating the
red train is the highest honor in the
north. As one commentator famously
notes, "Without Norway, we are just
skiers. against Norway. We are part of a
saga.
They don't treat winter as an obstacle
to overcome. They treat it as a home to
be cherished. And in 2026, the machine
isn't just looking for gold. It is
looking to prove again in the tracks.
There is no substitute for the long lung
and the quiet heart.
A dynasty is not measured by a single
season. It is a culture that stays
standing when the weather turns. The
Norwegian Olympic ski team does more
than collect metal. They teach the world
what sport looks like when an entire
country lives the same rhythm. A story
forged in forest [music] trails
organized through a century of festivals
proven on the hardest tracks in the
world. Challenged by the fierce cisu of
Finland and the relentless ambition of
Sweden. And even when trials hit, the
foundation holds. Because the foundation
isn't a myth, it is a habit. So today,
with cowbells still echoing through the
valleys of Lilahhammer and trails still
filling under the dark northern sky, the
Norwegian Olympic ski team is hereby
inducted into the Hall of Teams.
In the Hall of Teams, legends don't just
live in the past, they rise again
through you. This year, you decide who
earns the title team of the year. It's
more than a title. It's a legacy without
borders across all sport, across time,
across the world. We want to hear your
thoughts. So, let us know in the
comments what you liked in this journey.
When you liked and subscribe, you're not
just a viewer, you are part of the hall.
Each January, your voice helps decide
which legendary team claims the ultimate
title of team of the year. Help us
immortalize the greatest across all
sports, across time, and across the
world. Subscribe and make your voice and
your vote count.
Next time, we trade the silence of snow
for the scream of an engine and head to
Italy, where speed is never enough. We
follow Scrider Ferrari, forged in
post-war hardship and obsession from
Marinelo to the loudest circuits on
Earth. Because some teams chase
trophies, Ferrari chases immortality.
Until then, remember, behind every logo
is a legacy. Behind every team, a tale
we're telling. I'm Kirk Jensen, and this
is the Hall of Teams.

videos.title: KGB Productions on Freedom Riders: The Movie
videos.description: Terrorists or Freedom Fighters? Jackson Hole's KGB Productions susses it out in Freedom Riders: The Movie.

videos.title: 1966/70 Skiing in the early days featuring Dag Aabye hot dogging, Whistler, BC
videos.description:
1966/70 Skiing in the early days featuring Dag Aabye hot dogging, Whistler, BC

The footage likely has 1966 footage as well. (updated)

23 Dec update - A delight to see how popular this video has become in less than a week. There may possibly be a few more well known Canadian and European skiers in the footage. Working on more details, please feel free to let me know you find. 

Dag Aabye is the fellow in the Yamaha sports wear demonstrating a bit of hot dogging on the slopes. Dag Aabye (born 1941) is an endurance runner known for competing in the 125-kilometer Canadian Death Race, held yearly in August in Grande Cache, Alberta. The race is extremely grueling, with over 17,000 feet of climbing. Now 82 and living the hermit lifestyle near Vernon BC, he was a young man in his mid 20s during this time at Whistler. 

In the 1960s, Aabye was a ski instructor in Britain and worked as a movie stuntman, including working as an extra in the 1964 James Bond film Goldfinger. He soon after moved to the Whistler Mountain area of Canada where he worked as a ski instructor and became known for his first ski descents of the mountain, sometimes 20 to 30 years before anyone else. He was also known for stunts, skiing off man-made structures and building a ski jump for back-flips, and appearing in a number of ski movies by filmmaker Jim Rice. Pelletier and Maruniak assert that "Dag is widely regarded as the founding father of free-skiing." He has been described as "the Father of Free Ride" and "The Last Ski Bum".

A surprising bit of history. Enjoy!

17 min 29 secs, 16mm 2k digital scan
Kodachrome PCR 597

Episode map - 
https://bit.ly/Map-guide
_______________________________________ 

80+ hours original programming. Reel Life, PTOCS & CanadaMotorSports

Launched in 2010, Canadamotorsports has specialized in auto racing videos, all original footage primarily in Western Canada with the occasional interesting foray elsewhere. Reel Life now features vintage film footage and contemporary video of all varieties including motorsports, 30+ countries,  historical events and more. 16mm amateur film footage from 1923 to 1983.   

Vintage film stock footage - https://www.pond5.com/artist/vintagestockshooter#1/2063
Stock footage - https://www.pond5.com/artist/stockshooter

episode 1036
